segments.text: Hi, I'm Seth Freiberg, head trader of SMB Capital's Options Trading Desk here in Manhattan.
And today I'm going to be showing you how options traders at prop firms make money as professional traders.
And you know, when I tell people that I'm a professional trader and that the way I generate income and build wealth is by trading options,
people give me this look like this kind of polite hesitancy.
And I know that they're thinking, hey, this guy is just gambling. He's just depending on luck.
And he's just been lucky so far and when his luck changes, he's in for a world of hurt.
And you know, it'll probably shock you to hear me say this, but in a way, that statement's true.
Trading is in a way gambling and the market is in a way a giant legal casino.
Why would I say that? After all, I am a professional trader myself and I make a living trading.
So why would I say something like that? Well, let me ask you this very simple question.
At a casino, who are the participants that always lose money? And the answer is the players, the gamblers who come to the casino,
the customers of that casino. You see the gamblers, the customers, sometimes they'll win, sometimes they'll lose,
and sometimes they'll go on a sustained winning streak. And that of course is all based on pure luck.
But luck doesn't last forever. It eventually runs out. And 99 times out of 100,
they will end up losing everything they made during the lucky winning streak. And if they're lucky, they'll break even.
So then you ask yourself, well, who always makes money in a casino?
There is one party that always wins at a casino. And the answer is that the casino itself always wins in the end.
I'm sure you've heard this phrase, the house always wins. In other words, the casino always wins because no matter what happens,
they will be the winners in the end. Why do you think the casinos give away free food, free entertainment, free drinks,
just to attract people to gamble? Because the more the people gamble, the longer they gamble, the more money they gamble,
the more money the casino makes. And why is that? How can this casino assure itself that in a game of chance that they're making available to you,
that they are guaranteed to win no matter what? And so today, we're going to share with you the secrets of the casino business model.
We're going to show you how as a professional trader, you can set yourself up as a casino.
Because that's how professional options traders set themselves up to make money in the end. And so if that's a venture stick around because I think you're going to find this eye opening.
How do casinos guarantee that they will always make money in the end? Well, what they do is that they rig the game in such a way that they have a statistical edge over the player.

In other words, they have rigged the game such that they have what's called a positive expectancy. While the player, the gambler, the casino's customer, has a negative expectancy. So here's how it works.
So for example, on this roulette wheel, as you can see, the ball has landed on number 23, which is a red number.
Red 23, that's right. Come on.
Or if it had landed on 35, it would have landed on a black number and so on and so forth. So here's how it works.
Let's take a typical game like roulette. And in roulette, what happens is that you've got this giant roulette wheel.
Right. And this wheel is made up of numbers. And you've got this spinning device.
Okay. And when the crew be the guy who operates the roulette wheel, when he throws the little ball onto the wheel, the ball will spin around and around.
And it eventually lands on one of the numbers. So it's either an odd or even number or alternatively you can play the red or black numbers, whichever you like.
And if you win, you get the amount you bet. In other words, you double your money. And if you lose, you lose the full amount of your bet.
And so let's say that you decided to bet on black or red. So for example, 23 is a red number. 35 is a black number and so on and so forth.
Okay. And so people think, hey, this isn't bad. We can make a bet with the casino. And I've got a 50 50 chance of being right.
But unfortunately, they're wrong. It's not a 50 50 chance. And that's because the casino has rigged the game in such a way that there are 18 red numbers and 18 black numbers.

Or for that matter, 18 even numbers and 18 odd numbers. But regardless of whether you're playing the red black or the odd and even, there are two green numbers, which are the zero and the double zero.
And these slots are neither black nor red nor even or odd. And so therefore that is the secret.
So for example, if you bet on black, you think your chance of winning is 50 50 because there are an even number of red and black numbers on the wheel.

But that's not a 50 50 proposition because the equation isn't 18 out of 36. It's 18 out of 38 because there are 36 black and red numbers or alternatively 36 odd and even numbers plus two green numbers.
So the casino's chance of winning is 20 out of 38 because if for instance you bet on black and the ball lands on one of the 18 reds or one of the two greens, the casino wins as well.
So if you work it out, the player's chances of making money is about 47.3% and the casino's chances of making money is 52.7%.
So the casino's edge over the player is 52.7 minus 47.3, which is 5.4%. So the casino has a 5.4% edge over the player. And that's how they make all the money at the end of the day.

So what does that 5.4% represent? Well, it means that in the long run over many, many, many bets, billions of bets even for every $1 that people bet in the long run that casino will make 5.4% of that.

5.4% for every dollar bet. And so if a million dollars are bet on the roulette wheel every day, the casino will make on average $54,000. So the more people that play, the more people that bet, the more money the casino makes.

That's the simple reality or we can explain it in another way. For example, if the casino's got a thousand bets and each bet is a thousand dollars and you bet on red statistically out of a thousand bets, the casino is going to win 52.7% of the time.

The player is going to win 47.3% of the time, right? Which means the casino will win 527 bets and they'll lose 473 bets, right? Now each time there's a bet at the casino if the player loses the casino wins a thousand and if the player wins the casino loses a thousand.

So statistically after 1,000 bets, they'll win 527, they'll lose 473. So if you do the math, they'll make 527,000 dollars after a thousand bets and they'll pay out 473,000 on the losing bets, meaning they'll make 54,000 dollars. So that's how it works.

So what we're saying here is that as professional options traders, what we're actually doing is replicating the business model of the casinos and we're going to show you exactly how we do that in a minute. But first of all, let me focus on a very important topic.

And that is that most options traders lose money. 90% of people who trade options or even stocks or currencies, 90% of them lose money. And the reason most people will lose money is that they don't have a plan, they don't have a strategy, right? They trade based on rumors, based on emotions, based on opinions that they can't back up with any real logic or proof.

Because the reality is that most people who buy options lose money at the end of the day on those options. And the reason that they do is they do not understand the statistical basis of how options work.
So for example, let's take Hasbro stock Hasbro the toy maker was trading on December 1st at 8269. It's high for the year as a matter of fact. And so this trader heard that Hasbro is going to rally.
And he's heard that the cheap way to take advantage of a rally is to buy call options on a stock. So he heads over to an options chain which expires on January 16th, which is 46 days later.
And he buys an 87.5 call, which was trading for $1.40 that day. And so because the stock option represents 100 shares of stock you pay, a cash price actually of $140 for the call that day.
And that seems pretty reasonable because we think the stocks are going to go up in a rally to 87.5 would only be a rally of $4.81 a share.
So that's not much of a move for stock like that. So hey, if this thing is going to rally, I could make big bucks for only having to show out $140 for the right to buy 100 shares at 87.5 and 46 days.
That's plenty of time for this stock to take off and go way past 87.5 and then I'd be I'd be rolling in it.
But you'll notice that we're pointing to the Delta column for that options chain, which is a column you'll see on your broker platform.
And if you look at the column, you'll notice that the 87.5 call has a delta of 29.47, which basically means that the stock statistically actually has only a 29.47% chance of closing above 80.
And a half by January 16, which means that there is a 70.53% chance that the stock will not close above 87.5 that day.
And if you know anything about options, you'll realize that call options that are higher than the stocks closing price on the day that those call options expire.
They expire worthless, meaning you lose 100% of your investment in that option. So you lose $140 if that happened.
And you want to know the most frustrating part, the most frustrating part is that Hasbro actually did rally.
And by January 16, it closed at 86.2. But the call strike price was higher than that up at 87.5.
And so that option expired completely worthless and you lost $140.
And so you heard a bullish rumor, you were right, you bought a call option, the stock went up and you still lost money on the trade.
You lost all of your money on the trade. And you know why? Because you were basically just gambling.

You didn't understand the dynamics of options. You didn't understand their probabilities. You just ran out and based on a rumor, bought a cheap call option when you honestly didn't know what you were doing or the math behind options.
And so what you would have realized is that what was a likely winning trade was in reality rigged against you from the beginning.
Like the casino, the options market had rigged the game so that you had a 70.53% chance that you'd walk away with nothing and you didn't even know it.
And that's one of the major reasons that people lose money trading options. Even if they are right and a stock goes up in many cases like this example of Hasbro.
You see as professional traders, we make money at the end of the day by replicating the business model of the casino and not by replicating the foolishness of the gamblers.

Do you remember that we explained how casinos rigged the game in order to have a statistical edge over the player? Well now we're going to teach you how professional options traders rigged their trades legally, of course, through professional options trading strategies.
Okay, so most amateur options traders act like the guy we just mentioned who entered into Hasbro options trade with no real understanding of his probabilities or how he would win that trade.
Well, professional options traders don't fall into that trap instead. What we do is we study option strategies and we're keeping an eye out for repeatable strategies.
Strategies we can trade the same way every time that a trade is triggered by our indicator or whatever we're using to determine bullishness or bearishness.

And the key is that we will only adopt a strategy if we've been able to rigorously test it using years of data that this options trading technique has significant edge just like the significant edge that the casino has and we need to determine that that edge has been consistent and sustainable for many years.
And if a particular option strategy does not prove to have that historical edge, then we simply won't trade that strategy.
We won't take a random untested shot and risk our capital without proof that this trade over the long haul has edge.
That would be irresponsible. In fact, that would be gambling and we're not gamblers. We're traders.

So let me give you an example of what I mean. We have a trader on our trade desk who after hundreds of hours of back testing had found the strategy that was consistently profitable over many, many years.
Now, this is a proprietary strategy and it wouldn't be fair to the trader on our desk who did all that work to disclose the exact details of the strategy.
But what I'm going to be doing is the next best thing and that is to show you the way his strategy works.
The only thing you won't know is exactly what triggers the trade which only goes off if the market is very likely to make a pretty strong move in one direction or the other.
And the trigger will tell him whether it's likely to be a big bullish move or a big bearish move.

So through the example of what this trader developed, we're going to show you the principles of how professional traders work very hard to develop systems that allow them to trade like a casino and give themselves a sustainable edge.
And so to give you an example, let's move back to March 29, 2018.
And as you can see, the S&P 500 index which trades under the symbol of SBX had closed that day at 264087.

And so first thing in the morning on that next trading day which was April 2nd, 2018, the index opened at 263427 down a little bit from the previous close and the trader's trigger for a bearish trade will that went off.
And so he pulled up an SBX index option chain expiring 11 days later, which is part of his protocol.
And he moves down to the put side and he scans down that column called delta, which you'll find on any options platform.
And he looks for a put option with a delta of at least 75.
So he picks that 2700 put, which has a delta of 76.61, which is close.
And he buys that one at a price of $77 and 25 cents.
And simultaneously he heads down to the closest delta to the 25 delta, which is the 2565 call.
And he goes ahead and sells that one at a price of $1640.
Now when you do this, buying a put, hire up on an option chain and selling a put lower down on that same options chain.
When you do that, you are entering into what options traders refer to as a put debit spread, which is the option strategy this trader uses for this system when he gets a bear signal.
Now first of all, let's take a look at the cash needed to enter this trade.
You see he paid a price of 7725 for that 2700 put option and index options have a payoff of $100 per point.
And so you multiply that by 100. And so the cash cost of that 2700 put was $7,725.
But he also sold that 2565 put.
And for that, as you can see, he received cash from the amount of 1640.
And so the net cost of the trade was 6,085.
And so if we move to the end of the day, we can see that the index did sell off that day closing at 2581, 88.
Now let's say that the trader's protocol is to close the trade if it reaches a profit equal to 15% of the cost of the debit spread.
And conversely, he'll close the trade at a loss if the spread loses value such that it's lost 7.5% of its value.
So in other words, his target profits 15% of the capital he put into the trade and his max losses stop is 7.5%.
And so in this day, it so happens that 50% of the cost of the trade comes to $912.75.
So that's our profit target that day.
And so as it happens at 1015 AM that day, the 2700 put had rallied to price of 91.50.
And the 2565 put had also rallied to a price of 2060 because both puts are going to go up when the index is selling off.
And so the way that you close a debit spread is to sell off the put you bought and buy back the put you sold.
So if you had done that at 1015 AM that day, as you can see from the calculation, you'll see that the proceeds from closing the trade is $7,090.
Which as you can see results in a profit of over 16%, which slightly exceeds the 15% target profit.
Okay, and so assuming that the trade used about $6,000 in capital every time it was triggered.

What he found was that if you put this trade on every time his indicator triggers, there was a high probability historically, not a guarantee, but a really good probability that if the indicator tells him that it's going to be a bullish day,

then a bullish debit spread would be used, what's known as a call debit spread. The move would happen and that it will be a big enough move that he's going to hit his 15% profit target that day before the end of the day in all probability.
Or if it's a bear state like example, we just gave you there's a good probability that the put debit spread will achieve a profit of 15%.
Now, there's by no means a 100% chance of winning the trade, right? It could become a losing trade, but the probability is definitely greater than 50, 50 that will be one.
And in fact, he found from back testing many years that there was actually a 60% chance of it hitting its target profit before the end of the day.
And only a 40% chance that the trade would not make that move and instead go the opposite way.
And so if this happens, which it will, 40% of the time, he'll simply take a stop and close the trade when it gets drawn down to a loss of 70%.
So to reiterate, his target is 15%, which on an average trade of $6,000 comes to $900.
And his max losses stop is 70% or $450.
Okay, so let's say that as we said, 60% of the time you win and 40% of the time you lose.
Well, for one thing you'll notice right away that this is way better than the casino edge we described earlier where the casino only had 52.7% as its edge.
And there are numerous options strategies with wind rates well above 60%.
So you could argue that based just on the probabilities that his system is better off than a casino's roulette system.
But it gets better.
You see what makes this strategy special is the next part.
And you could argue the most important part.
You see when the casino offers a roulette game, the bet is that when you win, you win one to one on what you bet.
So if you bet $1,000 at a roulette wheel and you win, you get $1,000.
But if you lose, you lose $1,000.
But with this option strategy and many others actually, when you win, you get double what you lose.
And specifically as we said when the trader hits his target profit, he makes a profit of 15% of his capital that day or $900.
And our losing days, which of course are going to happen, he only loses 7.5%, which is $450.
In other words, when his trade gets drawn down to the point, words down 7.5% of the capital he put into the trade.
Then he's going to pull out and with discipline, close that trade.
And so in other words, his target profit, which is 15% of the capital he has in the trade, is double his stop or his max loss of 7.5%.
Or putting it another way he has intentionally set his stop at half of his profit target.
So on each winning trade, he wins double what he loses on losing trades.
In other words, we're risking a dollar to make $2.

Now what our trader has found is that if you do this consistently, in other words for example, if the trigger over time went off 100 times and therefore we entered 100 trades, you should win about 60 trades and you'll lose 40 trades.
But the crucial point is that each time I lose, I lose a dollar while each time I win, I win $2.
Why? Because like we mentioned, we fixed our stop loss and our target profit so that my risk reward is 1 to 2.
So if I'm my first trade that requires $6,000 in capital, I'll be risking 7.5% if you think about it or $450.
And suppose I lose that trade.
Well, if I win the next trade, which my historical stats show me will happen 6 out of 10 times or 60% then that next day when I win, I'll make $900.
And so for the first two days, I'm up $450 having $1900 and only lost $450.
And of course I know that whenever I make a trade, I've got no certainty as to whether it's going to be a win or a loss.
And in fact, in the short term, I may have a losing streak or for that matter, I might have a winning streak but either of those outcomes, well, that's what they call luck.
And it's basically just noise. But over many trades, say 100 in this example, in other words, over statistically significant number of trades, I know that the statistics are in my favor.
That I'll make $900,000, hitting my target profit at 15% more than half the time.
And the other times, I'm only losing $450, which is my 7.5% stop.
And so let's suppose that I want to try to figure out how much this system should make me for over 100 trades.
Well, that's the final part of this lesson, which pulls the whole thing together.
You see, every trading strategy has a win rate and a loss rate. And those are percentages, of course.
But they also have a dollar amount, which the amount of money you win when you win and the amount of money you lose when you lose.
And so what professional traders do is they have a formula called the expectancy formula.
And it tells you how much you expect to make on each trade over time.
And then you just multiply that expectancy per trade times the number of trades.
And the answer is how much you should make on the strategy over, say, 100 trades.
And so the expectancy formula works like this. You take the percentage probability of winning.
And you multiply that by the profit you make on the winning trades. And you subtract from that the probability of losing the trade times the amount you lose when you lose.
And so when you do that subtraction, the result is what is called the average outcome of the trade.
And if this is a positive number, then it's known as a positive expectancy strategy.
And if it's a negative number, it's known as a negative expectancy strategy.
And so for instance, with this trade, if you multiply the 60% probability of winning times the dollar amount you win as you can see from the calculation, you get a result of $540.
And if you multiply the 40% probability of losing by the amount you lose, you get a figure of $180.
So then by subtracting those two, you get what traders refer to as the average outcome of the trade or for short the expectancy per trade.
And so therefore over 100 trades, the result should be a profit of $36,000.
Now, there is no guarantee of course that you're going to win 60% of the time.
For instance, suppose that for whatever reason you go through a rough patch and your win rate drops down to even as low as 50%, which shouldn't happen.
But if it's somehow a did, then it means that out of 100 trades, you win 50, but you also lose 50, right?
Because of the way that we have set our risk to return ratio, we would still win $2 for every $1 we lose.
So we simply recalculate our expectancy calculation, assigning a 50% probability to both the winning and the losing.
In which case we still make $225 per trade on average.
And as you can see, even though each trade has only been a 50, 50 proposition, which means that our 100 trade expectancy would be a profit of $22,000, it's still a solid profit.
So in other words, if our win rate dips, we have a strong margin of error for an excellent outcome in either case.

And so what I'd like you to take away from today's video is that professional options traders develop trading systems that not only have a high probability of winning a trade, but also have a superior ratio of risk to reward, which acts as a kind of fail safe.
So if the probability of the trade starts to drop somehow, then the strategy should still be very profitable on the strength of the tight risk management, where we risk $1 to make $2,
meaning your risk to reward can actually drop to $1 to $1 and you'll still have a solid system.
This is how professional options traders discipline themselves to design systems that are safe and effective.
And now you've got the tools to begin taking a look at your own trading approach and fine tuning them to the standards we've described in this video.
If you can put that work in and achieve that, you may well have what it takes to be a professional options trader.
So thanks for watching and we'll see you next time.

Now if you'd like to learn three more options strategies that our pro traders use, including the unique options trick that allows you to make money while you wait to buy stocks or ETFs at the price you want, and the options income strategy that allows you to make consistent money, whether the market goes up or down or sideways, and how to make money on a stock or index trade,
even if you're wrong on the direction, then click the link that's appearing right now at the top right hand corner of your screen.
That will open up the free workshop registration page in a new window.
So don't worry you won't lose this video or you can register directly for free at options class.com.
